---
title: "Python Tip: lru_cache"
subtitle: "Speed up expensive function calls with caching"
description: "A quick tutorial on using functools.lru_cache with Fibonacci examples and cache management."
author: "Shep Bryan IV"
date: 2026-04-14
categories: [python, tutorial]
tags: [python, performance, caching, functools, lru_cache]
format:
  gfm: default
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Python Tip: `lru_cache`

Want to speed up your code by caching function results?
Use `functools.lru_cache` to store results of expensive function calls and reuse them.

## Basic Example of a Slow Function

Here is a slow recursive function for Fibonacci numbers. Without caching, it recalculates the same values many times.

:::{.code-animation}
```{python}
def slow_fibonacci(n):
    print(f"- Calculating Fibonacci({n})")
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    return slow_fibonacci(n - 1) + slow_fibonacci(n - 2)

x = slow_fibonacci(4)
print(f"Final result = {x}")
```
:::

## Using `lru_cache`

You can speed this up by adding the `@lru_cache` decorator. It caches results and returns them instantly when the function is called with the same arguments.

```{python}
from functools import lru_cache

@lru_cache(maxsize=None)  # No limit on cache size
def fast_fibonacci(n):
    print(f"- Calculating Fibonacci({n})")
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    return fast_fibonacci(n - 1) + fast_fibonacci(n - 2)

x = fast_fibonacci(4)
print(f"Final result = {x}")
```

## Cache Management

`lru_cache` stores results in memory, not on disk. Be careful when caching values that consume a lot of memory.

You can clear the cache manually and inspect cache stats like hits, misses, and current size.

```{python}
fast_fibonacci.cache_clear()  # Clears the cache
print(fast_fibonacci.cache_info())
```

## Animation Smoke Test

If this box pulses and the label changes to "JS loaded", then animation and inline JavaScript are working in your preview.

```{=html}
<div id="anim-smoke" class="anim-smoke">Waiting for JS...</div>
<style>
  .anim-smoke {
    display: inline-block;
    padding: 0.6rem 1rem;
    border-radius: 0.5rem;
    color: #fff;
    background: #2b6cb0;
    animation: anim-smoke-pulse 0.8s ease-in-out infinite alternate;
    font-weight: 600;
  }

  @keyframes anim-smoke-pulse {
    from { transform: scale(1); opacity: 0.7; }
    to   { transform: scale(1.06); opacity: 1; }
  }
</style>
<script>
  document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function () {
    var el = document.getElementById("anim-smoke");
    if (el) {
      el.textContent = "JS loaded";
    }
  });
</script>
```

## Wrap-Up

Now you can use `lru_cache` to speed up expensive function calls by reducing redundant computations.

Follow me for more tips.  
Shep Bryan IV
